text: The surgeon begins the operation by incising the peritoneum on the right side, medial to the right ureter, at the sacral promontory level. The incision is deepened to reach the presacral fascia, developing an avascular plane between the presacral fascia and the rectum using blunt and sharp dissection. The hook dissection extends to the bottom of the sacral hollow, continuing beyond the coccyx tip until the pelvic diaphragm fibers are visible. The dissection proceeds bilaterally, dividing the right lateral ligament with a hook, potentially using tissue sealants like ligature or harmonic. The anterior peritoneal reflection of the rectum is preserved.

The surgeon completes the dissection by dividing the right lateral attachments of the rectum, then performs similar dissection on the left lateral side. Attention is directed to the peritoneal reflection at the lateral pelvic wall, where the peritoneum is incised from top to bottom. Using careful hook dissection, the incision is deepened layer by layer until the left ureter is visible and the sacral hollow is accessed, ensuring the ureter is safeguarded and the correct plane is maintained.

The surgeon straightens the rectum and sutures it to the sacral promontory using 2-0 polypropylene sutures, applying traction to ensure proper alignment. The sutures are placed from the rectum to the presacral fascia at the sacral promontory, resulting in the rectum standing straight upon completion.
